original_abstract:
Developing lead-free relaxor ferroelectrics that simultaneously exhibit high recoverable energy density ($W_{rec}$) and high storage efficiency ($\eta$) is a critical objective for next-generation pulsed power applications. While local compositional heterogeneity is known to drive the formation of complex polar structures in solid solutions, its specific role in tailoring energy storage properties has remained largely unexplored. In this work, we report the achievement of an exceptional $W_{rec}$ of 15.2 J cm⁻³ and an ultrahigh $\eta$ of 91% in $Bi_{0.5}Na_{0.5}TiO_3-BaTiO_3$-based relaxors through the deliberate engineering of local chemical clustering.

By integrating density functional theory (DFT) calculations with three-dimensional atomistic models derived from neutron/X-ray total scattering and reverse Monte Carlo simulations, we identify sub-nanometer clustering of Bi, Na, and Ba. These clusters host distinct polar displacements, creating a unique polar state characterized by small clusters with significant fluctuations in the length and orientation of unit-cell polar vectors. Macroscopically, this translates to enhanced high-field polarizability, suppressed hysteresis, and superior breakdown strength. Furthermore, these structural features result in an unusual field-dependent increase in efficiency, excellent thermal and frequency stability, and high discharge capacity. This study highlights how modulating hidden local chemical order can govern polarization characteristics, providing a robust pathway for designing superior dielectric energy storage materials.
humanized_abstract:
Although great efforts have been made to discover lead-free relaxor ferroelectrics with enhanced recoverable energy density (W rec) and storage efficiency (η), which are crucial for pulsed power applications, little is known about the local compositional heterogeneity and the corresponding roles of the complex polar structures in the solid solutions. In this work, we demonstrate that precise control of local chemical clustering leads to a lead-free relaxor ferroelectric material with a record recoverable energy density of 15.2 J cm(-3) and storage efficiency of 91%, while keeping high stability of the properties.

We have linked density functional theory (DFT) calculations to 3D atomistic models derived from neutron/X-ray total scattering and reverse Monte Carlo simulations to elucidate the origins of the high performance of Bismuth sodium barium titanate (BNT)-based materials. We demonstrate that Bi, Na and Ba cluster into sub-nanometer-sized entities while the different polar displacements that are associated with these entities lead to a novel polar state of matter associated with small clusters of atoms showing large fluctuations in the length and direction of the unit cell polar vector and thus high field polarizability, low hysteresis loss and high breakdown electric field. This new state of matter is responsible for the field dependent increase in the efficiency of these materials, as well as their high thermal and frequency stability and high discharge capacity. Thus, by adjusting the hidden local chemical order of the materials, it is possible to control the polarization characteristics of these dielectric materials and as such they constitute a new generation of materials for dielectric energy storage applications.

We have linked density functional theory (DFT) calculations to 3D atomistic models derived from neutron/X-ray total scattering and reverse Monte Carlo simulations to elucidate the origins of the high performance of Bismuth sodium barium titanate (BNT)-based materials. document.input:
Developing lead-free relaxor ferroelectrics that simultaneously exhibit high recoverable energy density ($W_{rec}$) and high storage efficiency ($\eta$) is a critical objective for next-generation pulsed power applications.
